coredata.dc:title: Bio-derivatized and silver modified carbon dot based nanocomposite in multiple mode detection, catalytic reduction, and biocidal applications
coredata.dc:creator: Korah, Binila K.
K, Sreekanth
K, Radhakrishnan E.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) were prepared hydrothermally from Hemigraphis colorata leaves for the first time. It was then used for the fabrication of composite based on silver nanoparticles (CD-AgNP). Bio derivatization of nanomaterials has various advantages, including availability, low cost, and minimal toxicity. The composite was used extensively to accomplish five major applications. It detected cephalexin with exceptional selectivity and sensitivity using a fluorescence recovery-based method. Rhodamine B (RhB) and malachite green (MG), two polluting dyes, were catalytically reduced by the composite in seconds with a high degradation efficiency. The composite easily detected mercuric ions as a colorimetric probe, enabling mercury identification with the naked eye. With an extremely low limit of detection, the composite was also able to detect mercuric ions electrochemically. Both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria were successfully inhibited by the antibacterial activity of the CD-AgNP composite. The mechanisms in each application have been elaborated using appropriate analysis. This is the first investigation on the production of CDs and CD-AgNP composite using Hemigraphis colorata leaves. This work stands out because the proposed composite was fully exploited to its maximum potential. In a nutshell, all the aforesaid applications could be effectively carried out using a single nanocomposite that was produced using a fairly simple green strategy with a strong commitment towards sustainable environment.

head.ce:title: Bio-derivatized and silver modified carbon dot based nanocomposite in multiple mode detection, catalytic reduction, and biocidal applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass derived carbon dots (CDs) were prepared hydrothermally from Hemigraphis colorata leaves for the first time. It was then used for the fabrication of composite based on silver nanoparticles (CD-AgNP). Bio derivatization of nanomaterials has various advantages, including availability, low cost, and minimal toxicity. The composite was used extensively to accomplish five major applications. It detected cephalexin with exceptional selectivity and sensitivity using a fluorescence recovery-based method. Rhodamine B (RhB) and malachite green (MG), two polluting dyes, were catalytically reduced by the composite in seconds with a high degradation efficiency. The composite easily detected mercuric ions as a colorimetric probe, enabling mercury identification with the naked eye. With an extremely low limit of detection, the composite was also able to detect mercuric ions electrochemically. Both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria were successfully inhibited by the antibacterial activity of the CD-AgNP composite. The mechanisms in each application have been elaborated using appropriate analysis. This is the first investigation on the production of CDs and CD-AgNP composite using Hemigraphis colorata leaves. This work stands out because the proposed composite was fully exploited to its maximum potential. In a nutshell, all the aforesaid applications could be effectively carried out using a single nanocomposite that was produced using a fairly simple green strategy with a strong commitment towards sustainable environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The processed data required to reproduce these findings are available to download from [supplementary material].

ce:section.ce:para: The serendipitous discovery of carbon dots (CDs), slowly and steadily turned to one of the most researched topics in the world [1,2]. The properties, synthesis methods, applications of CDs were widely explored and have received great attention. The synthesis of CDs from biomass is currently trending due to the biocompatibility, abundance, availability and low cost [3–5]. High dispersibility in water, fluorescence, chemical stability, non-toxicity, and sustainability of the CDs have made them applicable in a multitude of areas [6–8]. CDs are recently applied in the stabilization and reduction of metal nanoparticles owing to the presence of surface functionalities with the potential to accept and donate electrons [9–12]. The extreme toxicity and biological risks possessed by chemical reducing agents can be avoided through the use of CDs [13]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are used in the material, energies, and sensing industries because they are less expensive than other noble metal nanoparticles and have excellent catalytic, optical, and antibacterial properties [14,15]. Different techniques, including organometallic grafting, ion exchange, and hydrothermal deposition followed by reduction, are used to create the carbon-supported AgNPs. To achieve perfect interaction between the carbon surface and metal precursors, a careful optimization of carbon surface properties is necessary in all approaches [16]. Recent research has shown that AgNPs can be made by reducing metal salts with the use of cheap plant extracts, fruit juices, and vegetables, where the natural compounds serve as a downsizing and crown agent [17–19]. Bio derivatization of nanomaterials have several advantages such as availability, inexpensive nature, and low toxicity [69–71].

The discharges from diverse sources, such as hospitals, municipalities, factories, and agricultural fields, are one of the main causes of environmental contamination. Numerous toxic species, including unmetabolized drugs, insecticides, herbicides, dyes, and metals that are discharged after fulfilling their primary function, are present in these effluents. Due to their various toxic effect to organisms that are not intended recipients, drugs that have been released into the aforementioned effluents in their unmetabolized form are classified as environmental pollutants. The presence of any sort of pollutant remains such as dyes, metal ions etc., in food and surface water has adverse effects on microbial community, lowering the quality of water and animal products [20,21]. The occurrence of such contaminants in wastewater has both direct and indirect negative impacts on human health, necessitating the development of quick, easy-to-use, analytical procedures for their identification.

Cephalexin is one of the antibiotics that is most frequently supplied [22]. For the detection of cephalexin, a number of techniques have been used, including solid phase extraction, chromatography, etc [22–26]. However, these methods require a lot of maintenance, intricate procedures, extended analysis times, and expert technicians. For routine and quick screening procedures, the aforementioned aspects are quite unsatisfactory. Another persistent pollutant, mercury, does not degrade in the environment; instead, it enters the food chain and reaches humans through the intake of marine goods, thereby leading to serious illnesses [27]. This points out to the need of efficient methods and materials which are quick, green and environment friendly. Rhodamine B (RhB) and malachite green (MG) are regarded as organic pollutant dyes which are highly mutagenic and harmful to all living beings. Elimination of these pollutants from water is necessary fulfill the ever-increasing demand for sustainable water. Traditional methods for treating waste water, such as adsorption, biodegradation, ultrafiltration, electrochemistry, and oxidation, cannot be used because of their numerous drawbacks, such as high costs, difficult reaction conditions, and lengthy time requirements. Thus, the construction of metal nano catalysts as a trustworthy technique for the reduction of dangerous organic pollutants becomes extremely important from an industrial and environmental standpoint. The actual purpose of our work is to provide a single resource that could address all of the aforementioned problems.

In this work, we developed a novel carbon dot modified silver nanoparticle (CD-AgNP) using a simple heating method without the aid of any sort of instruments. Carbon dots and silver nanoparticles have several properties and applications. Numerous investigations are still being conducted in these areas. The composite was developed as a result of the search for novel characteristics and advantages on combining these two major components. That was the intention behind this combination. Carbon dots have been widely used in fluorescence and electrochemical sensing applications, proving their utility in these disciplines. It is mostly because of their inherent fluorescence and electron transport properties. Silver nanoparticles have been shown to have antibacterial properties, colorimetric detection, and catalytic reduction. A hybrid composite of these two basic components possesses the capability to better precisely assign each of these characteristics. Here, carbon dot was synthesized hydrothermally from Hemigraphis colorata leaves and was used for the fabrication of the proposed composite. The formation of CDs and composite was confirmed through the extensive analysis of various characterizations. The composite was mainly applied for five major applications for the first time. (i) It detected cephalexin through a fluorescence recovery-based process with excellent selectivity and sensitivity, (ii) the composite was able to catalytically reduce pollutant dyes (RhB and MG) within a few seconds with high degradation efficiency, (iii) as a colorimetric probe, the composite detected mercuric ions within a fraction of seconds which facilitated the mercury identification through naked eye, (iv) the composite also detected mercuric ions through an electrochemical method with a very lower limit of detection, and (iv) antibacterial activity of the CD-AgNP composite was found successful for gram positive and gram negative bacteria.

This work is distinctive in a number of ways. This is the first study on the synthesis of CDs using Hemigraphis colorata leaves, as far as we are aware. This broadens the knowledge on the materials research. Even though there has been various carbon dot modified silver nanoparticles synthesized before, this work stands out because the proposed composite was effectively exploited to its maximum potential. There are hardly any studies in the literature on the fluorescence probe for cephalexin, electrochemical probe, and colorimetric probe for Hg(II) detection based on CD-AgNP composite. Also, the study uses a biomass precursor and uses no complex apparatus, making it easy and environmentally friendly.

Hemigraphis colorata leaves were obtained from the nearby garden. Metal salts employed were lead(II) nitrate, manganese(II) chloride, ferrous sulphate, copper(II) chloride, mercury(II) chloride, potassium dichromate, zinc sulphate, nickel(II) chloride, cadmium chloride and cobalt chloride. Amoxicillin, cephalexin, kanamycin, vancomycin, levofloxacin, doxycycline, tetracycline, paclitaxel, cloxacillin, fluorouracil are the antibiotics employed. Others used comprise rhodamine B, malachite green, silver nitrate, sodium borohydride, sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate and quinine sulphate. Double distilled water was used during the experimentation. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were bought from Merck, India.

A Perkin Elmer-400 spectrometer was used to collect FT-IR spectra. Shimadzu UV-2450, a Japanese instrument, was used to examine the UV–visible spectrum. A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was used to generate the photoluminescence spectra. To get the XRD, an X′PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer was employed. We used a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope to capture HR-TEM images. A scientific nanoparticle analyzer by Horiba, model SZ-100, was used to perform the DLS and zeta. The XPS investigation was carried out using an ESCALAB spectrometer from Thermo Scientific. The Raman spectrum was captured using a WITec Alpha 300 RA confocal Raman microscope with AFM. A three-electrode setup on a Biologic SP 200 workstation has been used for all electrochemical studies. The reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, the counter electrode was a Pt wire, and the working electrode was a gold electrode (AuE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CD-AgNP composites
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were prepared from Hemigraphis colorata leaves using hydrothermal method. The optimization details are illustrated in Fig. S1a and b. A simple and green approach was adopted for the synthesis of CD-AgNP composite. The procedure for the preparation of composite is as follows. 10 ml of bare carbon dot solution was diluted to 100 ml with distilled water. It was then boiled at 100 0C for 1 h. To the boiling solution 10 mM AgNO3 solution was added dropwise in dark, the solution was allowed to stand for the next 30 min with continuous stirring. The reddish-brown colored solution obtained was then allowed to cool to room temperature and then stored at low temperature ( Scheme 1). Optimization data is included in Fig. S1c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for fluorescence sensing of cephalexin
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of cephalexin (2–40 µM) were prepared. The fluorescence intensity was monitored for the CD-AgNP solution (1.6 mg/ml) containing each concentration of cephalexin. Selectivity studies and interference studies were performed using other drugs with the same concentration as that of the cephalexin (0.001 M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for catalytic reduction of dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The two pollutant dyes that were selected for the catalytic reduction using the proposed composite were rhodamine B (RhB) and malachite green (MG). In a typical procedure, 2 ml of RhB/MG (1 mM) and 1 ml of sodium borohydride solution were taken in a cuvette and the prepared composite (1.6 mg/ml) was added to the solution. The catalytic reduction of the dyes was observed using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. The control experiment, without the catalyst was also performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for colorimetric sensing of Hg(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: The following steps were used to determine Hg(II) ions using spectrophotometry. An aliquot (100 µL) of the required concentration (10–50 μM) of Hg(II) solution was added to a sample vial that already contained 3 ml of 1.6 mg/ml CD-AgNP solution. The solution was stirred for one minute, then let to stand for a short while before the absorption spectra was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for electrochemical sensing of Hg(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical sensing of mercuric ions was performed using CD-AgNP modified gold electrode (AuE). This was done by drop casting CD-AgNP solution onto the surface of bare AuE followed by air drying. Cyclic voltametric (CV) analysis of bare AuE, CD-AgNP coated AuE and the modified AuE in the presence of Hg(II) ions were recorded at 50 mV/s scan rate in the range − 0.2 V to 0.5 V. Selectivity study was performed with other metal ions in the concentration of 0.001 M using CV analysis. Differential Pulse Voltametric (DPV) technique was employed for determining the limit of detection. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy was also conducted for the bare and modified electrode.

Antibacterial activity of CD-AgNP composite against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli was evaluated by standard well diffusion in Mueller Hinton agar plates in triplicates by following the CLSI guidelines. The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of CD-Ag NPs was further determined against S. aureus and E. coli by using the 96 well microtiter plate assay.

Carbon dots were synthesized using hydrothermal method from Hemigraphis colorata leaves for the first time. The optimized temperature and time were 200 0C and 7 h, respectively (Fig. S1). Absorption spectrum of CDs exhibited two peaks at 274 nm and 333 nm corresponding to the π - π * and n - π * transitions, respectively. This arises due to the presence of CC and CO on the surface of carbon dots ( Fig. 1a). The obtained blue fluorescence of CDs was confirmed by the emission at 440 nm when excited at 355 nm. Excitation dependent spectrum was obtained with the maximum emission when excited at 355 nm (Fig. 1b). The uneven size distribution of the resulting CDs is implicated for this phenomenon. The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated to be 13.2 % (SI). Lifetime of the synthesized CDs was found to be 4.52 ns (Fig. S2a). The surface of carbon dots is rich in different functional groups, which contributes to the solubility of CDs in water. The FTIR spectra, which is shown in Fig. 1c, was recorded in order to interpret those functional groups. The presence of -O-H, -CO, C-N and C-O groups were confirmed by the peaks at 3304, 1571, 1401 and 1078 cm−1, respectively [28–31].

Following a detailed examination of the characteristic optical properties, transmission electron microscopy and XPS analysis were also used to study the morphological aspects. The information acquired from TEM images encompasses the spherical morphology, slight crystallinity, and average diameter ( Fig. 2). The fringe width was 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, and the average diameter was 2.3 nm that was clear from the size distribution histogram. Diffused rings in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirms that the carbon dots are not highly crystalline in nature (Fig. 2b). This is in agreement with the broad peak obtained in the XRD pattern of bare carbon dots (Fig. S5a). The average diameter obtained from the TEM distribution histogram matches with the result obtained from DLS experiment (Fig. S2b). The presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen was confirmed by XPS analysis, and the type of bonding was validated by high resolution spectrum analysis (Fig. S3). Stability studies of the synthesized CDs at varied conditions were evaluated and found that the CDs were stable at different ionic conditions, pH, prolonged photoirradiation, and upon storage for a long period (Fig. S4).

For the fabrication of stable CD-Ag nanoparticle composites, the synthesized CDs were employed as reducing and stabilizing agents. The FT-IR spectrum indicates that the surface of CDs contains some functional groups with reducing activity, such as hydroxyl. As a result, in the interaction with electron-withdrawing substances, CDs can operate as the electron donors. Additionally, the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs has a good attraction for silver ions. Therefore, these ions are most likely adsorbed by carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. CDs can therefore function as stabilizing agents to inhibit the aggregation of the generated composites. To confirm the formation of CD-AgNP composite, different characterization techniques were employed. Initially, the absorption spectrum of the composite was taken and analyzed ( Fig. 3a). A new peak centered around 440 nm was obtained that confirmed the surface plasmon resonance of silver nanoparticles [32]. Another observation that confirmed the composite formation was the diminishing of bright blue fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3b). This happened due to the induced transfer of electrons because of the interaction between CDs and silver nanoparticles [33]. The oxygen containing functional groups that are responsible for the fluorescence emission could be covered with silver nanoparticles in the composite formed [34,35].

Comparison of the FT-IR spectra of the bare CDs and composite also confirmed the formation of CD-AgNP composite (Fig. 3c). There was a decrease in the intensity of absorptions and shift in the values from 3304 to 3263 cm−1, 1571–1573 cm−1, 1401–1353 cm−1and 1078–1019 cm−1 which is an indication of the interaction between carbon dots and silver nanoparticles [36]. Raman analysis of the composite presented two major absorptions at 1371 cm−1 and 1564 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands of carbon nanomaterials, respectively (Fig. 3d). It is noteworthy that the two peaks in the Raman spectrum of bare CDs were deformed because of the intense fluorescence of CDs. But when the composite was formed, the fluorescence was quenched, which ultimately led to an enhanced Raman signal [37]. X-ray diffraction study is yet another means for understanding the crystalline nature of the synthesized composite. Since the silver nanoparticles have been formed on the surface of carbon dots, XRD pattern of the composite was very weak and difficult to distinguish. A comparative analysis of the XRD pattern of CDs and CD-AgNP composite is illustrated in Fig. S5a. The broad peak at 25.70 (002) of the carbon dots was shifted to 24.30 which confirmed the crystal growth of silver nanoparticle in the composite. The spike in interlayer spacing of CDs due to crystal growth resulted in the lowering of angle corresponding to the graphitic carbon [38,39]. The major peaks at 35.10, 38.30 and 55.70 are assigned to the (122), (111) and (142) planes of Ag nanoparticles (JCPDS No. 04–0783), respectively confirms the crystallinity of metallic silver (face centered cubic structure) in the CD-AgNP composite [40–42]. The strain in the crystalline structure, a feature of nanocrystals, is thought to be responsible for the slight alteration in diffraction peaks [43].

More investigations on the size and shape of the prepared composite were attained through the examination of TEM images. Well dispersed and spherical shaped nanoparticles with an average diameter of 8.8 nm were obtained ( Fig. 4a). To confirm the composite formation, HR-TEM images were analyzed and found that two distinct lattice fringes are present on the composite. The presence of silver nanoparticle was confirmed by the fringe width at 0.24 nm corresponding to the (111) plane of Ag which was also discovered from the XRD analysis [44,45]. Moreover, the presence of carbon dots on the surface of silver nanoparticles was revealed by the characteristic lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of carbon. And furthermore, direct evidence for composite fabrication is presented by the larger size and darker spots seen in the TEM images of the composite compared to the bare CDs. The darker silver nanoparticles are covered by carbon dots on their surface which are lighter in appearance. This has been confirmed through various characterization techniques. It is these CDs attached to the AgNPs that exhibited a shadow-like appearance. The small dots that appeared in the higher-resolution TEM image might be the unreacted CDs which are much smaller in diameter than the composite. EDX spectrum also confirms the presence of silver in the composite (Fig. S5 (c and d)). SAED pattern reveals the crystallinity of the composite, even though there is a slight decrease in the brightness of spots due to the presence of amorphous CDs on the surface of silver nanoparticles. The white dots in the SAED pattern reveals the metallic AgNPs and the three rings correlated to the (111), (122), and (142) lattice planes of silver.

The elemental composition and type of bonding present in the nanocomposite was confirmed from the XPS analysis. The survey spectrum of the composite ( Fig. 5a) exhibits four major peaks at 284, 368, 401 and 530 eV corresponding to the elements carbon, silver, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively. Deconvolution of C1s spectrum presented four peaks at 284.5, 285.5, 286.2 and 288 eV corresponding to CC, C-N, C-O and CO, respectively (Fig. 5b). The Ag 3d spectrum exhibited two peaks at 368.2 and 374.2 eV, which is assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 5c). Since the distance between the two maxima was approximately 6 eV, silver is present in the composite as Ag (0), as reported [46,47]. The atomic percentage of carbon and silver in the composite was found to be 60.05 % and 6.62 %, respectively. The effective fabrication of CD-AgNP composite was thus demonstrated via XPS analysis. Zeta potential analysis was also performed to get an idea on the stability and surface charge of the composite. The analysis also provides information on the structure of composites through a comparison of zeta potential values between CDs and the composites. The values were − 11.4 and − 44.2 mV for CD and CD-AgNP composites, respectively (Fig. S5b). The negative charge corresponds to the functional groups present on the surface of CDs which was confirmed from the FT-IR and XPS analysis. As clear from the values, there is an increase in the negative charge for the composites compared to the bare CDs. This clearly demonstrates that there are more carbon dots attached to silver nanoparticle surfaces, which explains the higher zeta potential value. In addition to confirm the existence of the composite, it also provides some insight into its structure and high stability [36,48].

The blue fluorescence of the carbon dots was quenched considerably upon composite formation. This is attributed to the electron transfer that occurred between the carbon dots and silver nanoparticles. This fluorescence that was quenched by the silver nanoparticles was recovered by the addition of cephalexin, thereby pointing towards a CD-AgNP based fluorescence sensor for the drug, cephalexin. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence of the composite increases linearly with increase in concentration of cephalexin. There exists a linear relation between the concentration of cephalexin and F/F0 values over a linear range of 0–40 µM (Fig. 6b). The limit of detection was found to be 0.24 µM which was considerably lower than other sensors ( Table 1). Selectivity of the sensor is an important factor for consideration. The fluorescence intensity of the composite did not vary significantly in the presence of other drug species, but in the presence of cephalexin the intensity increases considerably as depicted in Fig. 6c. Particularly, hardly any interference was seen while trying to detect cephalexin even if other drugs were present in the same quantity (Fig. 6d). The effect of pH (3−12) on the sensing of cephalexin was also tested, but there was negligible effect which led us to the performance of sensing at neutral pH (Fig. S6a). Based on these findings, it is concluded that the composite performs well as a fluorescence probe with excellent selectivity and sensitivity.

There are mainly two reasons for the fluorescence recovery process that led to the detection of cephalexin. Carbon dots is having a hydrogen bonding interaction between its surface functionalities and with those of the drug. Also, it is reported that the silver nanoparticles have a specific interaction with the cephalexin molecules [51]. Thus, a synergistic effect of both the factors lead to the immediate fluorescence recovery upon the addition of cephalexin. This was clear from the observation that on adding the same concentration of cephalexin the fluorescence intensity of composite increased considerably than bare carbon dots (Fig. S6b and c). On adding cephalexin molecule, the silver nanoparticle leaches out from the surface of composites and binds with cephalexin. Now, the CDs are free giving out a bright blue fluorescence. Upon increase in the concentration of cephalexin, CDs interact with cephalexin through hydrogen bonding and results in enhancement in the fluorescence. The entire mechanism is well illustrated by a detailed comparison of the FT-IR spectra of CD, CD-AgNP, and CD-AgNP/Cephalexin (Fig. S6d). An increase in the intensity of the CD-AgNP/Cephalexin systems demonstrates the dissociation of composite components following cephalexin addition. The shift in values for the hydroxyl group in the CD-Cephalexin system proved that there was hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and the cephalexin. Additionally, the change in peak values is a sign of interaction between cephalexin molecules and silver nanoparticles. The FT-IR spectral analysis therefore revealed that the addition of cephalexin molecules separates the composite to its components, causing composite disintegration, and the separated CDs and silver nanoparticles interact with the cephalexin, causing an enhancement in the fluorescence.

Zeta potential analysis has been used for additional reinforcement of the mechanism. Also, in the presence of cephalexin, the hydrodynamic diameter of the composite also dropped from 115.4 nm to 51.6 nm, which further supports the dissociation of the composite (Fig. S7a and b). The removal of CDs from the composite and the attachment of CDs and silver nanoparticles to the cephalexin molecules causes the negative charge of the composite to decrease from − 44.2 mV to − 32.4 mV after the introduction of cephalexin molecules (Fig. S7c). A schematic depiction of the proposed mechanism is shown in Scheme 2.

The catalytic potential of the prepared composites for the reduction of pollutant dyes was analyzed. Rhodamine B (RhB) and malachite green (MG) were the two dyes selected for the proposed application. RhB is a toxic and carcinogenic chemical compound that is frequently used as a dye. It has been used extensively in different sectors. RhB exhibits an absorption peak in its aqueous solution at 554 nm. The entire reduction process was observed by the changes in the peak intensity at 554 nm. According to Fig. 7a and b, the peak intensity was only slightly reduced on the addition of NaBH4 for a time period of 30 min, however when CD-AgNP composite and NaBH4 were present, the absorption peak intensity decreased dramatically until 95.5 % of RhB was reduced in 120 s. The amount of AgNPs was 0.018 g/ml for the catalytic reduction of rhodamine B and malachite green dyes. In the presence of CD alone, there was no change in the absorbance peak intensity. The reduction process executed pseudo first-order kinetics, as can be seen from graph plotted with ln(At/A0) against time, and the computed rate constant was 0.03265 s−1 (Fig. 7e). In the case of MG dye, there was no considerable difference in the major peak at 617 nm even after 60 min of prolonged irradiation, when only NaBH4 was present [52]. But in the presence of CD-AgNP composite, the peak declined considerably (85.6 %) within 120 s (Fig. 7c and d). The linear plot of ln(At/A0) against time confirmed pseudo first order kinetics and the rate constant was found to be 0.01467 s−1(Fig. 7f).

All the experiments were repeated for attaining accurate results. A comparative analysis of the catalytic reduction exhibited by the prepared catalyst is illustrated in Table 2. As it is clear from the table that, with the minimum amount of catalyst concentration, maximum efficiency was attained within a comparatively shorter duration of time interval. This demonstrates the superior ability of CD-AgNP composite in the catalytic reduction of pollutant dyes. The application of plant extract for nanoparticle fabrication is crucial since it is a simpler, easier, and less expensive technique. Proteins, alcohols, phenols, terpenoids, lipids, and other biomolecules found in plant extracts are involved in the reduction and capping of nanoparticles. As a result, the amounts of hazardous chemicals in the nanostructure are reduced. Thus, a non-toxic catalyst generated using green synthesis was used in the catalytic reduction of toxic dyes.

Rhodamine B and malachite green were the two dyes that were reduced using the prepared composites. In the presence of sodium borohydride alone, there was no notable change in the absorbance of dyes. But in the presence of the composite, reduction of pollutant dyes occurs significantly within a few seconds. The mechanism of reduction of pollutant dyes using CD-AgNP catalyst is as follows. Initially, the pollutant dyes get adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst, upon addition to the CD-AgNP-NaBH4 system due to the π-π interactions between aromatic rings of dyes and surface groups on CDs [53]. Electron transfer occurs between borohydride ions and silver nanoparticles and also between CDs and silver nanoparticles [54–56]. Finally, they will be transferred to the pollutant dyes resulting in their reduction within a few seconds. Electron transfer pathway and mechanism of reduction is illustrated in Scheme 3.

The composite catalyst designed was found successful for the application as a colorimetric sensor for mercuric ions. The significant difference in the absorbance of CD-AgNP composite on adding mercuric ions was easily visible through a color change from light yellow to colorless ( Fig. 8a). No such color change was observed for other metal ions pointing towards the selectivity of the sensor (Fig. 8b). The effect of other metal ions in the optical sensing of mercuric ions was also analyzed. The change in absorbance was almost constant even in the presence of other interfering substances with the same concentration (0.001 M) (Fig. S8a). The sensing showed a decrease at higher pH due to the formation of metal hydroxides (Fig. S8b). The effect of different concentrations of mercuric ions (10–50 µM) on the absorbance of composite was analyzed and observed a gradual decrease in the absorbance at 440 nm (Fig. 8c). The change was visible using the naked eye as the yellow color of the solution turned to nearly colorless. A linear calibration plot was obtained when the change in the absorbance was drawn against the concentrations of mercuric ions (Fig. 8d). The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.5 µM which was much lower than the previously reported silver nanoparticles ( Table 3). Thus, the modification of carbon dot increased the sensitivity of the sensor. The experiment was repeated using CD alone as the sensor, but there was no apparent color change pointing towards the importance of modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of colorimetric sensing of mercury
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism behind colorimetric sensing of mercuric ion is as follows. The surface of silver nanoparticles is enriched with CDs which contains several functional groups. The mercuric ions get attached around the composite surface through the CDs. This was clear from the following characterization techniques. The FT-IR spectral comparisons confirmed the interaction between mercuric ions and composite surface through decrease in the intensities and shift in band positions (Fig. S8c). A drop in the zeta potential value from − 44.2 mV to − 30.8 mV is also an indication of the bound mercuric ions (Fig. S8d). Another evidence that strongly supports the statement is the increase in the hydrodynamic diameter from 115.4 nm to 218 nm which was clear from the DLS analysis (Fig. S8e). CDs thereby supports the redox reaction between mercuric ions and silver nanoparticles. Mercuric ions get reduced and Ag(0) get oxidized resulting in the formation of colorless solution. The reduction potential of mercuric ions (0.86 V) is greater than silver ions (0.80 V), which resulted in the following redox reaction, 2Ag + 2Hg2+ → 2Ag+ + Hg2 2+ [57]. The reason for the selectivity of CD-AgNP composite towards the detection of mercuric ion is the lower reduction potentials of other metal ions [58,59].

The developed composite was coated on gold electrode (AuE) and was applied as an electrochemical probe for the detection of mercuric ions. The electrochemical behavior of the modified AuE/CD-AgNP towards the sensing of mercuric ions were analyzed in 0.1 M KCl solution over a potential range of − 0.2 V to 0.5 V. The cyclic voltammograms of bare AuE, AuE-CD, AuE/CD-AgNP, AuE/CD/Hg(II) and AuE/CD-AgNP/Hg(II) is shown in Fig. 9a. The modified electrode (AuE/CD-AgNP) produce redox peaks in the CV analysis due to the conversion between Ag(I) and Ag(0) whereas the bare electrode does not produce any [62,63]. When the electrode was modified only with carbon dots, there were no considerable peaks in the cyclic voltammogram. But in the presence of mercury small redox peaks appeared indicating the possibility of interaction between CD and mercuric ions. In the presence of Hg(II), the interaction between mercuric ions and modified electrode generates new peaks at 0.01 V and 0.05 V indicating the sensing potential of the developed electrode. Further confirmation about the sensing capability was made from the comparison of impedance spectra of bare AuE and AuE/CD-AgNP (Fig. S9). The modified one produced lower semicircle depicting the lower resistance or increased conductance which exhibits the superiority achieved with modification. Varying the scan rate from 30 mV/s to 150 mV/s resulted in a linear enhancement in the peak current corresponding to the surface-oriented transfer of electrons and reversible nature (Fig. 9b) [64]. The selectivity of the sensor towards Hg(II) ions was confirmed using the cyclic voltametric technique (Fig. 9c). The effect of concentration of mercuric ions in the electrochemical response of modified AuE/CD-AgNP was monitored using differential pulse voltametric analysis (Fig. 9d). A linear relation was observed between the peak current and concentration of mercuric ions and the LOD was calculated to be 0.004 nM within the range 1–8 nM (Fig. 9e). Compared to previously reported silver nanoparticles for the electrochemical detection of mercuric ions, the proposed sensor could detect Hg(II) ions with excellent limit of detection ( Table 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the electrochemical sensing of mercuric ions
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic interaction of CDs and silver nanoparticles in the composite rendered it possible to detect mercuric ions electrochemically using a gold electrode modified with CD-AgNP with excellent sensitivity. The oxidation and reduction of mercuric ions on the surface of the modified electrode correlates to the redox peaks that were assessed. Through an ion exchange process, the surface functions on CDs interact with the mercuric ions. There were no significant peaks in the cyclic voltammogram when the electrode was just treated with carbon dots. Small redox peaks did, however, arise when mercury was present, suggesting that CD and mercuric ions potentially interact. Silver nanoparticles have a lower reduction potential than mercuric ions [57]. This raises the mere possibility of a redox interaction between the two. CDs, thus stimulate the redox interaction between silver nanoparticles and mercuric ions [68]. The low redox potentials of other metal ions account for the selectivity of CD-AgNP composite towards the detection of mercuric ions.

CD-AgNP composites were observed to have antibacterial activity against both S. aureus and E. coli whereas CDs alone does not possess the biocidal activity. The zone of inhibition obtained for CD-AgNP composites was 20 and 17 mm, respectively against S. aureus and E. coli when used at a volume of 60 µL ( Fig. 10). By microdilution method, the MIC value of CD-AgNP composites was found to be 0.007 mg/ml and 0.014 mg/ml, respectively against S. aureus and E. coli. The potential of the nanocomposite to penetrate the microbial cells and a higher surface area for adhering to the bacterial cell wall inhibiting essential cell functions led to the biocidal application of the developed composite. The inhibition of CD and AgNP alone was studied. In the case of CDs, no inhibition was observed. But in the case of silver nanoparticles alone, the following result was obtained. An inhibition zone of 15 mm for S. aureus and 10 mm for E. coli. The hybrid composite, CD-AgNP enhances the antibacterial property due to its increased surface area attained upon composite formation. This helps the composite to penetrate the cell wall and inhibit cell functions.

In summary, carbon dot modified silver nanoparticle (CD-AgNP) composites were prepared through a simple heating technique using Hemigraphis colorata leaves as the carbon dot precursor. The carbon dot and obtained composite were characterized thoroughly using various characterization techniques. The blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched upon composite formation. The quenched fluorescence was recovered upon the addition of cephalexin thereby worked as fluorescence recovery-based probe for the detection of cephalexin with a detection limit of 0.24 µM over a linear range 0–40 µM. The composite also catalytically reduced two pollutant dyes within seconds obeying pseudo first order kinetics. The reddish-brown colored solution of the composite turned colorless in the presence of mercuric ions, thereby served as a colorimetric probe with a detection limit of 0.5 µM. Mercuric ions were also detected electrochemically with excellent selectivity and sensitivity (LOD = 0.004 nM) using the fabricated composite. It also exhibited biocidal activity against gram positive and gram-negative bacteria. Thus, a single composite that was developed utilizing a very simplistic green approach with an eye toward sustainability was able to effectively accomplish all the aforementioned applications.
Not applicable.

Binila K Korah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Sreekanth K: Software, Data curation. Radhakrishnan E. K: Visualization, Investigation. Beena Mathew: Supervision, Validation, Reviewing and Editing.
